content.problemStatement:
You are given a sequence of **nonnegative** integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_{N}A1​,A2​,…,AN​.

There is a pawn placed on the first element of the sequence, i.e. at position 111. When the pawn is on the position numbered with xxx, it can jump at most AxA_xAx​ places to the right. In other words, if Ax>0A_x > 0Ax​>0, the pawn can jump to any position from x+1x+1x+1 to x+Axx+A_xx+Ax​. If Ax=0A_x=0Ax​=0, the pawn can not move from the position numbered with xxx at all.

Find the maximum possible number of jumps using which it is possible to place the pawn on the last position, i.e. the position numbered with NNN, or determine that it is impossible to reach it.


Input Format


The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT - the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.




The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN.




The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.





Output Format

For each test case, print a single line containing one integer. In case it is possible to reach the last position, this integer should be the maximum possible number of jumps in which the pawn can be placed on the last position. Otherwise, this integer should be −1-1−1.
constraints: the sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 4⋅1054 \cdot 10^{5}4⋅105
0≤Ai≤N−i0 \leq A_i \leq N-i0≤Ai​≤N−i for each valid iii
the sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 400040004000

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Example case 1: The maximum number of jumps in a path from the position 111 to the position NNN is 111.
Example case 2: It is impossible to reach the position NNN because A1=0A_1=0A1​=0.
Example case 3: It is possible to reach the position NNN using 333 jumps if we follow the path 1→3→4→51 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 51→3→4→5.
Example case 4: It is possible to reach the position NNN using 555 jumps if we follow the path 1→2→3→4→5→61 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 5 \rightarrow 61→2→3→4→5→6.

Explanation:
Example case 1: It is possible to reach the position NNN using 222 jumps if we follow the path 1→5→71 \rightarrow 5 \rightarrow 71→5→7. It is impossible to reach it using 333 jumps because when the pawn is located at the position 333 it can not move anywhere.
Example case 2: It is possible to reach the position NNN using 111 jump if the pawn jumps straight to the position NNN from the position 111. Note that it must not jump to the position 222 because it can not jump anywhere from that position.
Example case 3: It is impossible to reach the position NNN.
